enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted app for cataloging, tagging, and sharing personal collections such as hobby items, cards, or memorabilia.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Koillection is a free, open-source, self-hosted platform for cataloging and organizing personal collections. Users can create collections and items, attach photos and details, and organize everything with flexible tags and custom 'signs' rather than rigid categories.

Because it runs on your own server, all uploaded data and images stay under your control. Koillection also supports wishlists, a REST API for custom integrations, and works as a Progressive Web App so it can be used comfortably from a phone or tablet browser.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Koillection suits hobbyist collectors — of trading cards, stamps, figurines, books, or any other physical items — who want a structured digital catalog without handing their data to a third-party service. It also fits anyone who wants to selectively share parts of a collection with others while keeping full control over what is visible.

features.title: Item & Collection Management
features.description: Create nested collections and catalog individual items with photos, custom fields, and notes.

features.title: Tags & Signs
features.description: Organize items flexibly using tags and custom 'signs' instead of fixed category trees.

features.title: Wishlists
features.description: Track items you want to acquire alongside your existing collection.

features.title: Controlled Sharing
features.description: Share collections with others while choosing exactly what they can see.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Access and manage collection data programmatically for custom scripts or integrations.

features.title: Progressive Web App
features.description: Use Koillection from a mobile browser with an app-like experience, no native app store install required.

pros: Completely free and open source
Full ownership of your data since everything lives on your own server
Flexible tagging system adapts to many collection types
Works well on mobile via PWA support
REST API allows custom integrations and automation
cons: Requires a self-hosting environment — no official managed cloud version
No native iOS or Android app, only the PWA
Smaller community than mainstream inventory or collection apps

faqs.question: Is Koillection free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Koillection is completely free and open source; you self-host it on your own server at no licensing cost.

faqs.question: Can I share my collection with other people?
faqs.answer: Yes. Koillection lets you share collections while controlling exactly what visitors can see.

faqs.question: Does Koillection have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: There is no native app, but Koillection works as a Progressive Web App that can be added to a phone's home screen.

faqs.question: Does Koillection have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it exposes a REST API for custom integrations and automation.

use_cases.title: Hobby collection cataloging
use_cases.description: Track trading cards, stamps, coins, or figurines with photos, notes, and custom tags.

use_cases.title: Memorabilia archiving
use_cases.description: Document and organize personal or family memorabilia with full data ownership.

use_cases.title: Shared community collections
use_cases.description: Selectively share parts of a collection with friends or collector communities.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Koillection is a free, self-hosted app for cataloging, tagging, and sharing personal collections with full data ownership.

enrichment.short_description: AI-based grammar, spelling, and style checker with browser extensions, desktop apps, and an open-source self-hostable core.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LanguageTool is a writing assistant that checks text for grammar, spelling, punctuation, and style issues across more than 20 languages. It began as an open-source grammar-checking project and is now also offered as a commercial product with browser extensions, desktop apps, and office integrations.

Beyond basic spellchecking, LanguageTool flags awkward phrasing, redundant words, and complex punctuation problems, and its Premium tier adds a paraphrasing tool that rewrites sentences to be more formal, concise, or fluent. The underlying open-source engine can also be self-hosted, letting developers run grammar checking on their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: LanguageTool is aimed at writers, students, non-native speakers, and businesses that need multilingual grammar and style checking across documents, email, and web forms. Developers who want a self-hosted, privacy-preserving grammar-checking API can run the open-source core directly.

features.title: Grammar & Spelling Check
features.description: Detects grammatical errors and misspellings as you type, across more than 20 languages.

features.title: Punctuation & Style Suggestions
features.description: Flags comma, dash, and hyphen issues along with overused phrases and redundant wording.

features.title: Paraphrasing Tool
features.description: Premium feature that rewrites sentences to be more formal, fluent, simple, or concise.

features.title: Browser Extensions
features.description: Available for Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari, and Opera.

features.title: Desktop Apps
features.description: Native apps for Windows and macOS check text outside the browser.

features.title: Office & Email Integrations
features.description: Plugins for Google Docs, Microsoft Word, LibreOffice, Apple Pages, Gmail, Outlook, and Thunderbird.

features.title: Open-Source Core
features.description: The underlying LanguageTool engine is open source and can be self-hosted as a local server.

pros: Generous free tier catches common spelling and grammar mistakes
Supports more than 20 languages
Wide platform coverage: browsers, desktop, and major office suites
Open-source core can be self-hosted for privacy-sensitive use
Premium adds a built-in paraphrasing tool
cons: Many advanced style and grammar checks are gated behind Premium
Month-to-month Premium pricing is significantly higher than the annual plan
Self-hosted core lacks some cloud-only Premium features
Like other grammar checkers, occasional false positives occur

faqs.question: Is LanguageTool free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free version covers basic spelling, punctuation, and some style mistakes. Premium unlocks additional error checks and the paraphrasing tool.

faqs.question: What does LanguageTool Premium cost?
faqs.answer: Individual Premium plans start around $4.99/month when billed annually, with quarterly and monthly options priced higher; team plans start around $9.48/month for two users.

faqs.question: Can I self-host LanguageTool?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source LanguageTool engine can be run as a local server, separate from the commercial cloud product.

faqs.question: What languages does LanguageTool support?
faqs.answer: LanguageTool supports more than 20 languages and dialects, including multiple varieties of English, German, Spanish, French, Portuguese, Dutch, and Catalan.

use_cases.title: Everyday writing improvement
use_cases.description: Catch grammar, spelling, and style mistakes in emails, documents, and web forms.

use_cases.title: Language learning support
use_cases.description: Get real-time corrections and explanations while writing in a non-native language.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted grammar checking
use_cases.description: Run the open-source engine on your own server for privacy-sensitive applications.

features: All grammar, spelling, and style checks

enrichment.seo_meta_description: LanguageTool checks grammar, spelling, and style in 20+ languages, with a free tier, Premium plans, and an open-source self-hostable core.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source machine translation API that can run fully offline and self-hosted, or via a paid hosted API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LibreTranslate is a free and open-source machine translation API, powered by the Argos Translate engine, that can be self-hosted and run entirely offline once its language models are downloaded. It supports translating text and files across a range of languages via a simple JSON API.

For teams that don't want to manage their own infrastructure, a hosted API is available through the LibreTranslate portal on Pro and Business plans, alongside a free, rate-limited public endpoint that doesn't require an API key.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: LibreTranslate fits developers and organizations that need translation capability without sending text to third-party cloud services — for example, apps handling sensitive documents, air-gapped environments, or products that want to avoid recurring API costs by self-hosting.

features.title: Self-Hosted Translation API
features.description: Deploy your own instance and translate text without relying on external services.

features.title: Offline Capable
features.description: Once language models are downloaded, translation works without an internet connection.

features.title: Language Auto-Detection
features.description: Automatically identifies the source language of submitted text.

features.title: File Translation
features.description: Translate documents, not just raw text strings.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Simple, documented HTTP API for integrating translation into apps and pipelines.

features.title: Hosted API Option
features.description: Managed Pro and Business plans available for teams that don't want to self-host.

pros: Free and fully self-hostable under the AGPLv3 license
Can run completely offline, keeping data private
Simple REST API that's easy to integrate
No API key required for the free public tier
Hosted plans available for teams that prefer not to manage infrastructure
cons: Translation quality can lag behind large commercial engines like Google Translate or DeepL for some language pairs
Self-hosting requires downloading models and provisioning compute
Free public tier is rate-limited (roughly 20 requests/minute, 2,000-character limit)
Supports fewer languages than major commercial translation APIs

faqs.question: Is LibreTranslate free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the software is free and open source, and you can self-host it at no cost. A free, rate-limited public API is also available without an API key.

faqs.question: Can LibreTranslate run offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, once you download the language models, a self-hosted instance can translate without an internet connection.

faqs.question: What does the hosted API cost?
faqs.answer: The managed LibreTranslate portal offers a Pro plan around $29/month and a Business plan around $58/month, with custom pricing available for higher volumes.

faqs.question: What license is LibreTranslate under?
faqs.answer: LibreTranslate is released under the AGPLv3 license.

use_cases.title: Privacy-sensitive translation
use_cases.description: Translate documents on your own infrastructure without sending text to third-party servers.

use_cases.title: Air-gapped or offline apps
use_cases.description: Provide translation capability in environments without internet access.

use_cases.title: Budget-friendly translation API
use_cases.description: Avoid per-call costs of commercial translation APIs by self-hosting.

features: Unlimited use on your own infrastructure

enrichment.seo_meta_description: LibreTranslate is a free, open-source, self-hostable translation API that can run offline, with an optional paid hosted service.

enrichment.short_description: Free, self-hosted vehicle maintenance and fuel mileage tracker with reminders, reports, and multi-vehicle support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LubeLogger is a self-hosted, open-source vehicle maintenance and fuel mileage tracker. It lets owners log service work with attached invoices or receipts, track fuel economy across multiple measurement standards, and set reminders based on either date or odometer reading.

A dashboard visualizes spending by timeframe and category, and a built-in planner organizes upcoming to-do items by status, priority, and type. Data can be exported and imported via CSV, and an API is available for custom integrations, all running on infrastructure you control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: LubeLogger suits vehicle owners who want a detailed, private maintenance history instead of relying on a third-party app or a paper logbook. It also works for small fleets or multi-vehicle households that want centralized records with multi-user access.

features.title: Service Record Tracking
features.description: Log maintenance work with attached documents such as invoices and receipts.

features.title: Fuel Mileage Tracking
features.description: Track fuel economy in MPG, L/100km, and other measurement standards.

features.title: Maintenance Reminders
features.description: Set alerts based on calendar date or odometer reading.

features.title: Planner
features.description: Organize upcoming maintenance to-do items by status, priority, and type.

features.title: Spending Dashboard
features.description: Visualize expenses by timeframe and category.

features.title: CSV Import/Export & API
features.description: Move data in and out of LubeLogger and integrate with other tools.

pros: Completely free and open source
Detailed multi-vehicle maintenance and fuel history
Flexible reminders based on date or mileage
Document attachments keep invoices and receipts with each record
CSV and API support for data portability
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge (e.g. Docker) to deploy
No official native mobile app, only a web interface
No built-in cloud backup — you're responsible for backing up your own instance

faqs.question: Is LubeLogger free?
faqs.answer: Yes, LubeLogger is free, open-source software that you self-host.

faqs.question: Can I track more than one vehicle?
faqs.answer: Yes, LubeLogger supports tracking multiple vehicles and multiple users.

faqs.question: Does LubeLogger have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: There is no dedicated native app; LubeLogger is accessed through a web browser, which works on mobile devices too.

faqs.question: Can I try LubeLogger before installing it?
faqs.answer: Yes, a demo with test credentials is available to explore before deploying your own instance.

use_cases.title: Personal vehicle maintenance logging
use_cases.description: Track service history, fuel economy, and expenses for a car or motorcycle.

use_cases.title: Small fleet record-keeping
use_cases.description: Centralize maintenance records for multiple vehicles with multi-user access.

use_cases.title: DIY maintenance documentation
use_cases.description: Attach invoices and receipts to a running record of self-performed work.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: LubeLogger is a free, self-hosted vehicle maintenance and fuel mileage tracker with reminders, reports, and an open API.

enrichment.short_description: Free, self-hosted spam protection for web forms that filters submissions by content instead of using CAPTCHA puzzles.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: mosparo is a self-hosted spam protection system for web forms. Rather than asking users to solve a CAPTCHA challenge, it checks the actual content submitted through a form — comparable to how an email spam filter works — using only the entered data plus the IP address and user agent, which are automatically hashed or encrypted.

The widget's design can be customized to match the look of the site it's embedded in, and submitted form data is automatically deleted after 14 days. mosparo is free, open source, and designed to be accessible to screen-reader users and anyone using a mouse, keyboard, or touch input.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: mosparo fits website owners and developers who want to block spam on contact, signup, or comment forms without burdening legitimate visitors with CAPTCHA puzzles, and who prefer to self-host their spam-filtering infrastructure rather than depend on a third-party service.

features.title: Content-Based Spam Filtering
features.description: Analyzes actual form submission content rather than requiring a CAPTCHA challenge.

features.title: Accessibility-Friendly
features.description: Works with screen readers and supports mouse, keyboard, and touch input without puzzles.

features.title: Privacy-Focused Data Handling
features.description: Only uses submitted form data plus IP address and user agent, with automatic hashing/encryption.

features.title: Customizable Widget
features.description: The protection widget's appearance can be adapted to match a site's design.

features.title: Automatic Data Deletion
features.description: Form submission data is automatically deleted after 14 days.

features.title: Self-Hosted Deployment
features.description: Runs on your own infrastructure, including via Docker.

pros: No CAPTCHA puzzles for legitimate visitors to solve
Strong accessibility support for screen-reader users
Privacy-focused with short, automatic data retention
Free and self-hosted, giving full control over form data
Widget design can be customized to match your site
cons: Requires self-hosting and ongoing maintenance
Smaller, newer project compared to established anti-spam services
Needs manual integration into existing forms or CMS platforms

faqs.question: Is mosparo free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, mosparo is free and open source; you self-host it on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: How is mosparo different from a CAPTCHA?
faqs.answer: Instead of making users solve a puzzle, mosparo checks the actual content of a form submission, similar to how an email spam filter works.

faqs.question: What data does mosparo store?
faqs.answer: It uses only the data entered into the form plus the IP address and user agent, which are automatically hashed or encrypted, and deletes submission data after 14 days.

faqs.question: Can mosparo be self-hosted with Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, mosparo supports Docker-based deployment on standard web hosting infrastructure.

use_cases.title: CAPTCHA-free contact forms
use_cases.description: Block spam on contact and signup forms without making visitors solve puzzles.

use_cases.title: Accessible form protection
use_cases.description: Provide spam filtering that remains usable for screen-reader and assistive-technology users.

use_cases.title: Privacy-first spam filtering
use_cases.description: Avoid sending form data to third-party CAPTCHA providers by self-hosting spam protection.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: mosparo is a free, self-hosted, accessible spam protection system for web forms that avoids CAPTCHA puzzles.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted virtual browser in Docker that streams a shared, remote browsing session over WebRTC with low latency.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Neko is a self-hosted virtual browser that runs inside a Docker container and streams its interface to participants over WebRTC, with latency of roughly 300ms or less. Multiple people can join the same session and take turns controlling the browser, making it useful for real-time collaboration.

Sessions can either persist across devices or be run as throwaway, isolated instances with no history, cookies, or cache retained locally. Because only video and audio are streamed to participants, the underlying browsing activity and network access stay on the server, which also lets Neko act as a jump host into internal resources or as a live broadcast source for platforms like Twitch and YouTube.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Neko fits developers pairing on debugging sessions, educators or streamers who want to broadcast a shared browser, teams needing a remote jump-host browser for internal resources, and privacy-conscious users who want disposable, local-trace-free browsing sessions.

features.title: WebRTC Streaming
features.description: Streams the browser interface and audio with near real-time latency, typically under 300ms.

features.title: Multi-Participant Control
features.description: Multiple people can join a session and share or hand off browser control.

features.title: Live Broadcasting
features.description: Stream the session directly to platforms like Twitch and YouTube via RTMP.

features.title: Persistent or Throwaway Sessions
features.description: Choose sessions that persist across devices, or isolated sessions with no retained history, cookies, or cache.

features.title: Jump Host Capability
features.description: Use Neko as a browser-based gateway to reach internal network resources remotely.

features.title: Browser Automation Support
features.description: Compatible with automation tools like Playwright and Puppeteer.

pros: True shared control for real-time browser collaboration
Low-latency WebRTC streaming (under ~300ms)
Disposable sessions leave no local trace on the client device
Can double as a jump host for reaching internal resources
Free, open source, and self-hosted via Docker
cons: Requires self-hosting infrastructure and Docker familiarity
Runs a full browser instance per session, which is resource-intensive
No official managed hosting option
Networking and authentication setup can be complex for some use cases

faqs.question: Is Neko free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Neko is free, open-source software that you self-host, typically via Docker.

faqs.question: What is Neko used for?
faqs.answer: It's used for shared remote browsing, collaborative debugging, live-streaming a browser session, and privacy-isolated browsing.

faqs.question: Can multiple people control the browser at once?
faqs.answer: Multiple participants can join a session, with the host able to manage who has control.

faqs.question: Does Neko keep my browsing history?
faqs.answer: You can run isolated, throwaway sessions with no retained history, cookies, or cache, or persistent sessions if you prefer.

use_cases.title: Remote pair debugging
use_cases.description: Share a live browser session with teammates to debug or demo something together.

use_cases.title: Live browser broadcasting
use_cases.description: Stream a shared browsing or watch-party session to Twitch or YouTube.

use_cases.title: Isolated privacy browsing
use_cases.description: Run disposable browser sessions that leave no trace on the local device.

use_cases.title: Internal network jump host
use_cases.description: Access internal web resources remotely through a browser running inside the network.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Neko is a free, self-hosted virtual browser that streams a shared, low-latency browsing session over WebRTC via Docker.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, ad-free collection of everyday web tools for images, PDFs, text, and data, processed entirely client-side.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OmniTools is a self-hosted collection of web-based utilities covering everyday tasks such as image, video, and audio conversion, PDF manipulation, and text and data-format helpers for formats like JSON, CSV, and XML. All processing happens on the client side, so files never leave the user's device.

The project ships as a lightweight Docker image (around 28MB), making it fast and simple to self-host, and it includes no ads or tracking. It's maintained as an open-source project under the MIT license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OmniTools fits developers, sysadmins, and privacy-conscious users who want a self-hosted alternative to the many ad-heavy public 'online tool' websites, especially for teams that want an internal, always-available utility suite without sending files to third-party servers.

features.title: Client-Side Processing
features.description: Files are processed entirely in the browser and never uploaded to a server.

features.title: Image, Video & Audio Tools
features.description: Convert and manipulate common media formats.

features.title: PDF Tools
features.description: Perform everyday PDF operations without third-party services.

features.title: Data Format Utilities
features.description: Work with JSON, CSV, XML, and other structured data formats.

features.title: Lightweight Docker Image
features.description: Deploys from a roughly 28MB Docker image for fast, simple self-hosting.

features.title: No Ads or Tracking
features.description: The self-hosted app runs without advertising or usage tracking.

pros: Extremely lightweight and fast to self-host
Strong privacy since file processing happens client-side
Wide variety of everyday utilities in a single app
Free, open source (MIT licensed), no ads or tracking
Simple one-command Docker deployment
cons: Requires self-hosting; no official hosted version for privacy-sensitive use is highlighted
Tool depth varies by category compared to dedicated single-purpose apps
Younger project with a smaller community than long-established tool sites

faqs.question: Is OmniTools free?
faqs.answer: Yes, OmniTools is free and open source, licensed under MIT, and self-hosted via Docker.

faqs.question: Do my files ever leave my device?
faqs.answer: No, OmniTools processes files entirely on the client side, so nothing is uploaded to a server.

faqs.question: What kinds of tools are included?
faqs.answer: OmniTools includes image, video, audio, PDF, text, math, date/time, and data-format (JSON/CSV/XML) utilities.

faqs.question: How do I install OmniTools?
faqs.answer: It can be deployed with a single Docker command using the official iib0011/omni-tools image.

use_cases.title: Private everyday conversions
use_cases.description: Convert images, PDFs, and data formats without uploading files to a public website.

use_cases.title: Internal team utility suite
use_cases.description: Self-host a shared toolbox of common utilities for a team instead of relying on ad-heavy public sites.

use_cases.title: Developer everyday tooling
use_cases.description: Quickly convert or inspect JSON, CSV, and other data formats during development.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OmniTools is a free, self-hosted collection of image, PDF, text, and data utilities that process files entirely client-side.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source weather API offering free non-commercial forecasts and historical data from 15+ national weather services.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (up to 10,000 non-commercial calls/day); commercial plans from $29/month

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Open-Meteo is an open-source weather API that combines more than 30 weather models from over 15 national meteorological services — including ECMWF, NOAA, DWD, and Météo-France — into a single, simple HTTP GET interface that requires no authentication for non-commercial use.

It also provides deep historical weather data via ERA5 reanalysis dating back to January 1940, with output available in JSON, CSV, or XLSX formats. The codebase is open source under AGPLv3, so organizations can self-host their own instance for unlimited calls, air-gapped environments, or high-volume machine learning workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Open-Meteo fits developers who want weather data in a hobby or non-commercial project without API-key setup, plus researchers, climate scientists, and machine learning engineers who need a long historical weather archive. Commercial weather applications can use paid plans with dedicated endpoints and a commercial license.

features.title: 30+ Weather Models
features.description: Aggregates data from 15+ national meteorological services with resolutions from 1-2 km to 9-11 km.

features.title: Historical Weather Archive
features.description: ERA5 reanalysis data back to January 1940, hourly and spatially complete.

features.title: No API Key for Free Tier
features.description: Non-commercial use requires only a simple HTTP GET request, no authentication.

features.title: Multiple Output Formats
features.description: Returns data as JSON, CSV, or XLSX.

features.title: Frequent Model Updates
features.description: Global models refresh roughly every 6 hours; regional models update every 1-3 hours.

features.title: Self-Hostable Codebase
features.description: Open-source under AGPLv3, allowing organizations to run their own instance.

pros: No authentication needed for free, non-commercial use
Combines many national weather models into one API
Deep historical archive going back to 1940
Self-hostable for unlimited or air-gapped use
Simple HTTP GET interface, easy to integrate
cons: Free tier is capped at 10,000 calls/day and limited to non-commercial use
Commercial use requires a paid subscription
Self-hosting the full model pipeline needs significant storage and compute
Attribution is required under the CC BY 4.0 data license

faqs.question: Is Open-Meteo free?
faqs.answer: Yes, for non-commercial use up to 10,000 API calls per day, no API key is required.

faqs.question: Can I use Open-Meteo commercially?
faqs.answer: Yes, via paid Standard, Professional, or Enterprise plans that include a dedicated endpoint and commercial license.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Open-Meteo?
faqs.answer: Yes, the codebase is open source under AGPLv3 and can be self-hosted for unlimited or air-gapped use.

faqs.question: How far back does historical data go?
faqs.answer: Historical data via ERA5 reanalysis is available from January 1940 onward.

use_cases.title: Weather features in apps
use_cases.description: Add forecast data to apps or websites without API-key friction for non-commercial use.

use_cases.title: Climate and ML research
use_cases.description: Pull long historical weather archives for research or model training.

use_cases.title: Commercial weather services
use_cases.description: Use dedicated, paid endpoints for production weather applications.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Open-Meteo is an open-source weather API with a free non-commercial tier, deep historical data since 1940, and paid commercial plans.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hostable text-to-speech reader that turns EPUB, PDF, DOCX, and other documents into synced read-along audio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenReader is an open-source document reader that turns EPUB, PDF, DOCX, TXT, and Markdown files into a synchronized read-along experience with multilingual text-to-speech and word-level highlighting. It uses layout-aware PDF parsing to preserve document structure and can export finished audio as an audiobook.

It supports multiple text-to-speech providers, including self-hosted, OpenAI-compatible TTS servers such as Kokoro-FastAPI and Orpheus-FastAPI, as well as cloud APIs like OpenAI, Replicate, and DeepInfra — so users can choose between a fully local, private setup or a cloud-based one.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OpenReader is well suited to people who benefit from read-along audio, such as those with dyslexia or visual impairments, and to self-hosters who want a private alternative to commercial text-to-speech reading apps for converting ebooks and PDFs into audio.

features.title: Multilingual Read-Along TTS
features.description: Reads documents aloud with synchronized, language-aware highlighting.

features.title: Broad Format Support
features.description: Works with EPUB, PDF, DOCX, TXT, and Markdown files.

features.title: Layout-Aware PDF Parsing
features.description: Detects structured blocks and stitches content across pages for accurate reading order.

features.title: Word-Level Highlighting
features.description: Synchronizes highlighting with audio playback for precise read-along tracking.

features.title: Multi-Provider TTS
features.description: Use self-hosted TTS engines (Kokoro-FastAPI, KittenTTS-FastAPI, Orpheus-FastAPI) or cloud APIs (OpenAI, Replicate, DeepInfra).

features.title: Audiobook Export
features.description: Export a document's narration as an audio file for offline listening.

pros: Can run with fully self-hosted, local TTS engines for privacy
Supports multiple document formats in one reader
Synchronized highlighting aids accessibility and focus
Flexible: bring your own TTS provider, local or cloud
Free and open source
cons: High-quality self-hosted TTS engines may need a GPU or significant compute
Setup requires configuring a TTS backend rather than a single-click install
Community-maintained project with no dedicated commercial support
Cloud TTS providers (OpenAI, Replicate, DeepInfra) incur their own usage costs if used

faqs.question: Is OpenReader free?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenReader is free and open source, and can be self-hosted.

faqs.question: What file formats does OpenReader support?
faqs.answer: It supports EPUB, PDF, DOCX, TXT, and Markdown documents.

faqs.question: Do I need an OpenAI API key?
faqs.answer: No, OpenReader can run entirely with self-hosted TTS engines like Kokoro-FastAPI, though it also supports cloud providers such as OpenAI if you prefer.

faqs.question: Can OpenReader run fully offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, when paired with a self-hosted TTS backend, OpenReader can operate without any external cloud calls.

use_cases.title: Accessible reading support
use_cases.description: Provide synchronized read-along audio for users with dyslexia or visual impairments.

use_cases.title: Private audiobook creation
use_cases.description: Convert personal ebooks and PDFs into audio using self-hosted TTS engines.

use_cases.title: Document-to-audio workflows
use_cases.description: Turn long documents like reports or research papers into audio for hands-free consumption.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenReader is a free, open-source, self-hostable TTS reader that turns EPUB, PDF, and DOCX files into synced read-along audio.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source zero trust networking platform that secures services by identity instead of IP address, sponsored by NetFoundry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenZiti is an open-source zero trust networking platform that replaces traditional IP-based network security with identity-based access control, encrypting all traffic end-to-end with libsodium-based cryptography. Services protected by OpenZiti can become effectively invisible on the network, resolved only through an authenticated private DNS rather than a public IP address.

The project offers flexible deployment models: embedding zero trust directly into applications via SDKs for the strongest security, using tunnelers for host-level zero trust access in existing environments, or covering network-level access where apps and hosts can't be modified. OpenZiti is sponsored by NetFoundry, which also offers managed and commercially supported self-hosted options built on the same open-source project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OpenZiti fits engineering and security teams looking to replace or augment traditional VPNs with a true zero trust model, and developers who want to embed zero trust access directly into their applications through an SDK rather than relying solely on network-level tunneling.

features.title: Identity-Based Access Control
features.description: Grants access based on cryptographic identity rather than IP address or network location.

features.title: Invisible Services
features.description: Protected services are not discoverable through normal network scans.

features.title: End-to-End Encryption
features.description: Uses libsodium-based cryptography to secure all traffic in transit.

features.title: Private, Authenticated DNS
features.description: Resolves service names to secure tunnels rather than exposing public IPs.

features.title: Embedded SDKs
features.description: Lets developers build zero trust access directly into applications (ZTAA).

features.title: Tunneler-Based Access
features.description: Provides zero trust host access (ZTHA) for existing apps without code changes.

pros: Genuine identity-based zero trust model, not just VPN rules on top of IP addresses
Fully open source with an active GitHub community
SDKs allow embedding zero trust directly into applications
Multiple deployment models fit different levels of application integration
Backed by NetFoundry with optional commercial support and managed hosting
cons: Steeper learning curve than plug-and-play consumer VPN tools
Production deployments often benefit from NetFoundry's paid support or managed tiers
Full protection requires embedding SDKs or deploying tunnelers across your environment
Self-hosting the full control plane requires networking and security expertise

faqs.question: Is OpenZiti free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the OpenZiti open-source project is free to self-host. NetFoundry also offers paid managed and commercially supported self-hosted options.

faqs.question: Who maintains OpenZiti?
faqs.answer: OpenZiti is an open-source project sponsored by NetFoundry.

faqs.question: What's the difference between ZTAA, ZTHA, and ZTNA?
faqs.answer: ZTAA embeds zero trust directly into applications via SDK for the strongest security, ZTHA uses tunnelers for host-level access in existing production environments, and ZTNA covers network-level access when apps or hosts can't be modified.

faqs.question: Does OpenZiti replace a VPN?
faqs.answer: It's designed to replace traditional IP-based VPN and firewall models with an identity-based zero trust approach, rather than simply tunneling traffic like a conventional VPN.

use_cases.title: Zero trust VPN replacement
use_cases.description: Replace traditional site-to-site or remote-access VPNs with identity-based zero trust access.

use_cases.title: Application-embedded zero trust
use_cases.description: Build zero trust access directly into an application using OpenZiti SDKs.

use_cases.title: Hiding internal services
use_cases.description: Make internal services invisible to network scanners and unauthorized access attempts.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenZiti is a free, open-source zero trust networking platform that secures services by identity, sponsored by NetFoundry.
